story:
Red lights pulse. Sirens howl. “Alert. Navigation failure. Proximity alert. Impact in thirty seconds.”

Captain Mara Voss snaps awake in her cryo-pod, lungs burning, voice rough. Around her, the crew jolts from sleep—Commander Juno Reyes, Pilot Kenji, Dr. Elara Nix, and Myra—groggy, blinking, already behind.

They demand a status update. Kenji checks navigation. They’re not near Tau Ceti. They’re not even anywhere the system recognizes. The nav map is blank, as if the place they’ve dropped into doesn’t exist at all. Myra points through the viewport at a planet swelling in size—impossible and undeniable. The computer cuts off their questions with a command: “Brace for landing!” Retro-rockets fire. The ship slams through atmosphere, metal screams, everything shakes.

Then silence.

Outside, the sky is lavender, stained with twisted orange clouds. Trees rise in sharp, angular lattices that chime in the wind like glass. The ground looks like a melted version of something familiar—Earth, but warped. They suit up and step onto glossy rock. The air reads thin, high-altitude, with a little under sixteen percent oxygen. Other gases don’t register. They keep helmets sealed.

Elara whispers that they’re not supposed to be here. No charts show a planet anywhere near. Kenji glances at his wristpad and watches two hours vanish in five seconds. Time isn’t behaving. They walk a hundred meters among drooping, softened shapes. Myra says it’s like a Dalí painting, the ones with melting clocks, except these aren’t clocks and it isn’t even hot.

A soft hum begins, barely there, then grows. They turn back toward their ship—and freeze.

Minutes ago, the vessel was intact. Now, in its place, stands a crumbling husk strangled by translucent vines, pitted, rusted, ancient. It looks like it’s been abandoned for years. Mara whispers what they’re all thinking: it’s like they’ve already been here, long ago, and left this relic behind.

The hum becomes a pulse under their boots. Instruments twitch. Commander Reyes taps her screen, jaw tight. They’re not merely off course. They’re caught in a loop, something folding time and place in on itself. The planet is wrong, the physics are wrong, and the map has nothing to say—because no map can hold this.

The ground trembles. The glassy trees answer with a voice—familiar, distorted, layered with static and memory. It’s their own voices, returned to them from somewhere ahead or behind.

“Welcome back,” the forest says.

In moments, the crew goes from mission routine to a fragile thread of logic. The ship has landed itself as if compelled. The sky bleeds colors they don’t have names for. Time slides past like oil. A wristpad loses hours; a starship gains years. The place acts as if it remembers them before they can remember it. They stand in a landscape that looks like a dream someone tried to hold too tightly—softened edges, drooping shapes, beauty bent strange.

They are explorers in a system that doesn’t exist, breathing caution through sealed helmets, counting seconds that won’t stay put. And the world speaks in their own voices, greeting them like old friends who never left.
